Recursion

Last updated: Apr 15, 2026

  • 0) Quick Reference

  • Quick Tips

  • 4-2) One Edit Distance (LC 161)
  • 4-3) Merge Two Sorted Lists (LC 21)
  • 4-4) Subtree of Another Tree (LC 572)

Recursion

0) Quick Reference

When should I use recursion?

  • When a problem has overlapping subproblems that reduce the scope
  • When you can clearly define a base case and recursive case
  • When the problem naturally decomposes into smaller instances of itself
  • For tree/graph traversal or backtracking problems

Quick Decision Guide

Use Case Pattern Key Idea
Need info from parent nodes Top-Down Pass context down while traversing
Need results from children Bottom-Up Solve children first, combine results
Need to split & merge results Divide & Conquer Partition problem, solve parts, merge
Need to explore all possibilities Backtracking DFS with decision making
Multiple recursive calls, same subproblems Memoization Cache results to avoid redundant work

Core Principle

For a problem F(X) where X is the input:

11. Break down into smaller scopes: x₀, x₁, ..., xₙ ∈ X
22. Recursively solve: F(x₀), F(x₁), ..., F(xₙ)
33. Combine results to solve F(X)

Quick Tips

  • When in doubt: Write down the recurrence relationship (how F(n) relates to F(n-1), F(n-2), etc.)
  • For redundant calls: Apply memoization (cache intermediate results)
  • For stack overflow: Use tail recursion or convert to iteration

1) Concepts

1-1) Core Ideas

1-2) Complexity Analysis

Time Complexity: Think of recursion as a tree structure:

1        fib(5)
2       /      \
3    fib(4)    fib(3)
4    /    \     /    \
5fib(3)  fib(2) fib(2) fib(1)
6 /   \    /  \   /  \
7fib(2) fib(1) fib(1) fib(0)
8 /   \
9fib(1) fib(0)

Given a recursion algorithm: O(T) = R × O(S)

  • R = number of recursion invocations
  • O(S) = time complexity of work per call
  • For Fibonacci without memoization: O(2^n) (exponential)

Space Complexity:

Recursion-Related Space (Call Stack):

  • Local variables in recursive function calls
  • Input parameters
  • Output variables
  • Stack overflow risk: when allocated stack space reaches system limit

Recursion-Independent Space (Heap):

  • Global variables
  • Memoization cache (stores intermediate results)
  • Important: Count memoization space when analyzing overall complexity

1-3) Related Concepts

Recursion is used in:

  • DFS (Depth-First Search) — tree/graph traversal
  • Backtracking — exploring all possibilities with pruning
  • Tree problems — natural fit for recursive algorithms
  • Dynamic Programming — with memoization optimization

2) Patterns

2-1) Basic Operation

Endless loop through elements in list (common in backtracking/generation):

1# Example: LC 22 (Generate Parentheses)
2_list = ["(", ")"]
3for x in _list:
4    _tmp = tmp + x
5    help(_tmp)

2-2) Top-Down Recursion

Definition: Start from the root and make decisions at each node based on information passed down from parent nodes. Also known as “preorder” approach.

Time Complexity:

  • Usually O(n) where n is the number of nodes
  • Can be O(n²) if same subproblems are solved repeatedly without memoization

Space Complexity:

  • O(h) where h is the height of recursion tree (call stack)
  • O(n) additional space if memoization is used

Use Cases:

  • When you need to pass information from parent to child
  • Tree traversal with accumulated state
  • Path-based problems
  • Validation problems

Pros:

  • Intuitive and easy to understand
  • Natural for problems requiring parent-to-child information flow
  • Good for early termination conditions

Cons:

  • May do redundant calculations without memoization
  • Can have higher space complexity due to call stack

Pattern:

1def topDown(node, parentInfo):
2    # Base case
3    if not node:
4        return baseResult
5
6    # Use parentInfo to make decision
7    currentResult = processWithParentInfo(node, parentInfo)
8
9    # Pass updated info to children
10    newParentInfo = updateParentInfo(parentInfo, node)
11    leftResult = topDown(node.left, newParentInfo)
12    rightResult = topDown(node.right, newParentInfo)
13
14    # Combine results
15    return combineResults(currentResult, leftResult, rightResult)

Common LeetCode Problems:

  • LC 104: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
  • LC 110: Balanced Binary Tree
  • LC 112: Path Sum
  • LC 113: Path Sum II
  • LC 124: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
  • LC 236: Lowest Common Ancestor
  • LC 257: Binary Tree Paths
  • LC 404: Sum of Left Leaves
  • LC 437: Path Sum III

Example - Path Sum (LC 112):

1def hasPathSum(self, root, targetSum):
2    def topDown(node, currentSum):
3        if not node:
4            return False
5
6        currentSum += node.val
7
8        # Leaf node check
9        if not node.left and not node.right:
10            return currentSum == targetSum
11
12        # Continue to children with updated sum
13        return (topDown(node.left, currentSum) or
14                topDown(node.right, currentSum))
15
16    return topDown(root, 0)

2-3) Bottom-Up Recursion

Definition: Start from leaf nodes and build up the solution by combining results from child nodes. Also known as “postorder” approach.

Time Complexity:

  • Usually O(n) where n is the number of nodes
  • Generally more efficient as each node is visited exactly once

Space Complexity:

  • O(h) where h is the height of recursion tree (call stack)
  • Usually no additional space needed for memoization

Use Cases:

  • When solution depends on results from subtrees
  • Tree property calculations (height, diameter, etc.)
  • Aggregation problems
  • Dynamic programming on trees

Pros:

  • More efficient - each subproblem solved exactly once
  • Natural for problems requiring child-to-parent information flow
  • Often leads to cleaner code
  • Better performance in most cases

Cons:

  • Can be less intuitive for some problems
  • May need to return multiple values from recursive calls

Pattern:

1def bottomUp(node):
2    # Base case
3    if not node:
4        return baseResult
5
6    # Get results from children first
7    leftResult = bottomUp(node.left)
8    rightResult = bottomUp(node.right)
9
10    # Process current node using children results
11    currentResult = processNode(node, leftResult, rightResult)
12
13    return currentResult

Common LeetCode Problems:

  • LC 104: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
  • LC 110: Balanced Binary Tree
  • LC 543: Diameter of Binary Tree
  • LC 124: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
  • LC 968: Binary Tree Cameras
  • LC 979: Distribute Coins in Binary Tree
  • LC 1120: Maximum Average Subtree
  • LC 1130: Minimum Cost Tree From Leaf Values
  • LC 1372: Longest ZigZag Path in a Binary Tree

Example - Maximum Depth (LC 104):

1def maxDepth(self, root):
2    def bottomUp(node):
3        if not node:
4            return 0
5
6        # Get depths from children
7        leftDepth = bottomUp(node.left)
8        rightDepth = bottomUp(node.right)
9
10        # Current depth is max of children + 1
11        return max(leftDepth, rightDepth) + 1
12
13    return bottomUp(root)

Example - Balanced Binary Tree (LC 110):

1def isBalanced(self, root):
2    def bottomUp(node):
3        if not node:
4            return True, 0  # (isBalanced, height)
5
6        # Check left subtree
7        leftBalanced, leftHeight = bottomUp(node.left)
8        if not leftBalanced:
9            return False, 0
10
11        # Check right subtree
12        rightBalanced, rightHeight = bottomUp(node.right)
13        if not rightBalanced:
14            return False, 0
15
16        # Check current node balance
17        isCurrentBalanced = abs(leftHeight - rightHeight) <= 1
18        currentHeight = max(leftHeight, rightHeight) + 1
19
20        return isCurrentBalanced, currentHeight
21
22    balanced, _ = bottomUp(root)
23    return balanced

Comparison Table:

Aspect Top Down Bottom Up
Direction Root → Leaves Leaves → Root
Information Flow Parent → Child Child → Parent
When to Use Need parent context Need subtree results
Efficiency May have redundancy Usually more efficient
Intuition More intuitive for path problems More intuitive for aggregation
Memoization Need Often needed Rarely needed

2-4) Pass State to Next Recursion

Pass accumulated state/context as parameters to child recursive calls. Useful when you need to track information from parent nodes.

Example: LC 404 (Sum of Left Leaves)

1// LC 404 - Sum of Left Leaves
2// IDEA: Pre-order traversal, pass isLeft flag to track if node is left child
3private int processSubtree(TreeNode subtree, boolean isLeft) {
4    // Base case: empty subtree
5    if (subtree == null) {
6        return 0;
7    }
8
9    // Base case: leaf node
10    if (subtree.left == null && subtree.right == null) {
11        return isLeft ? subtree.val : 0;
12    }
13
14    // Recursive case: process left and right subtrees
15    return processSubtree(subtree.left, true) + processSubtree(subtree.right, false);
16}

Key Insight: By passing isLeft as a parameter, we track parent context without needing global state.

2-5) Any-True Status in Recursion

When you need to find ANY true result among recursive calls, use OR logic. Stop early if any recursive call returns true.

Example: LC 572 (Subtree of Another Tree)

1// LC 572 - Subtree of Another Tree
2public boolean isSubtree(TreeNode root, TreeNode subRoot) {
3    // Check if subtree rooted at 'root' matches 'subRoot'
4    // Use OR: if ANY recursive call returns true, short-circuit and return true
5    return isSameTree(root, subRoot) 
6        || isSubtree(root.left, subRoot) 
7        || isSubtree(root.right, subRoot);
8}
9
10private boolean isSameTree(TreeNode node1, TreeNode node2) {
11    if (node1 == null || node2 == null) {
12        return node1 == null && node2 == null;
13    }
14    return node1.val == node2.val 
15        && isSameTree(node1.left, node2.left) 
16        && isSameTree(node1.right, node2.right);
17}

Key Insight: Using OR (||) allows early exit when a true result is found, avoiding unnecessary recursive calls.

2-6) Cartesian Product Construction

Definition: Recursively generate all possible structures by dividing a range, building all sub-results for each partition, and combining them via Cartesian product. This is a form of Divide & Conquer where the “combine” step enumerates all left × right combinations.

Time Complexity: O(4^n / n^(3/2)) — Catalan number growth

Space Complexity: O(4^n / n^(3/2)) — storing all generated structures

Use Cases:

  • Generate all structurally unique trees (BST, full binary trees)
  • Enumerate all ways to parenthesize/split an expression
  • Any problem where you partition a range and combine all sub-results

Pattern:

11. Pick each element i in [start, end] as the "root" / split point
22. Recursively build all left results from [start, i-1]
33. Recursively build all right results from [i+1, end]
44. Cartesian product: for each left × right, construct and collect result
55. Base case: empty range → return [null/None] (one empty result, NOT empty list)

1// Template: Recursive Construction via Cartesian Product
2private List<TreeNode> build(int start, int end) {
3    List<TreeNode> res = new ArrayList<>();
4    if (start > end) {
5        res.add(null);  // CRITICAL: null = valid empty subtree
6        return res;
7    }
8    for (int i = start; i <= end; i++) {
9        List<TreeNode> lefts = build(start, i - 1);
10        List<TreeNode> rights = build(i + 1, end);
11        for (TreeNode l : lefts)
12            for (TreeNode r : rights)
13                res.add(new TreeNode(i, l, r));
14    }
15    return res;
16}

Key Insight: Base case must return [null] (list containing null), NOT empty list. Otherwise Cartesian product loses all trees with empty left/right subtrees.

Optimization: Add memoization with Map<Pair<Integer,Integer>, List<TreeNode>> to avoid recomputing overlapping subproblems.

Common LeetCode Problems:

  • LC 95: Unique Binary Search Trees II
  • LC 96: Unique Binary Search Trees (Catalan count)
  • LC 241: Different Ways to Add Parentheses
  • LC 894: All Possible Full Binary Trees
  • LC 1382: Balance a Binary Search Tree

Example — LC 95: Unique Binary Search Trees II:

1def generateTrees(n):
2    if n == 0: return []
3    def generate(start, end):
4        if start > end:
5            return [None]
6        all_trees = []
7        for i in range(start, end + 1):
8            for left in generate(start, i - 1):
9                for right in generate(i + 1, end):
10                    root = TreeNode(i)
11                    root.left = left
12                    root.right = right
13                    all_trees.append(root)
14        return all_trees
15    return generate(1, n)

3) Advanced Techniques

3-1) Memoization

Idea: Cache results of recursive calls to avoid redundant work when the same subproblem is encountered multiple times.

When to Use:

  • When recursive calls repeat (overlapping subproblems)
  • When time complexity without memoization is exponential
  • Trade space for time (use a hash map for cache)

Example 1: Fibonacci

1# Without memoization: O(2^n) — exponential
2def fibonacci(n):
3    if n < 2:
4        return n
5    return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2)
6
7# With memoization: O(n) — linear
8def fibonacci(n):
9    cache = {}
10    def helper(n):
11        if n in cache:
12            return cache[n]
13        if n < 2:
14            res = n
15        else:
16            res = helper(n - 1) + helper(n - 2)
17        cache[n] = res
18        return res
19    return helper(n)

Example 2: Climbing Stairs (LC 70)

1# Without memoization: O(2^n)
2class Solution:
3    def climbStairs(self, n):
4        if n == 1:
5            return 1
6        if n == 2:
7            return 2
8        return self.climbStairs(n - 1) + self.climbStairs(n - 2)
9
10# With memoization: O(n)
11class Solution:
12    def climbStairs(self, n):
13        cache = {}
14        def helper(n):
15            if n in cache:
16                return cache[n]
17            if n <= 2:
18                res = n
19            else:
20                res = helper(n - 2) + helper(n - 1)
21            cache[n] = res
22            return res
23        return helper(n)

Reference: https://leetcode.com/explore/learn/card/recursion-i/255/recursion-memoization/1495/

3-2) Divide & Conquer

Template:

11. Divide: Split problem into subproblems
22. Conquer: Solve each subproblem recursively
33. Combine: Merge subproblem results

Pseudo-code:

1def divide_and_conquer(problem):
2    # (1) Divide
3    subproblems = divide(problem)
4    
5    # (2) Conquer
6    results = [divide_and_conquer(sub) for sub in subproblems]
7    
8    # (3) Combine
9    return combine(results)

Common Examples:

  • Merge Sort — O(n log n)
  • Quick Sort — O(n log n) average
  • Binary Search — O(log n)

Common LeetCode Problems:

  • LC 22: Generate Parentheses
  • LC 84: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
  • LC 315: Count of Smaller Numbers After Self
  • LC 493: Reverse Pairs
  • LC 1649: Create Sorted Array Through Instructions

Reference: https://leetcode.com/explore/learn/card/recursion-ii/470/divide-and-conquer/2869/

3-3) Recursion to Iteration (Unfold Recursion)

Why Convert:

  • Avoid stack overflow risk
  • Improve space/time efficiency
  • Reduce function call overhead

How to Convert:

11. Use a stack or queue to replace the system call stack
22. At each recursion point, push parameters onto data structure
33. Replace recursive chain with loop over the data structure

Example: https://leetcode.com/explore/learn/card/recursion-ii/503/recursion-to-iteration/2693/

4) Complete LeetCode Examples

4-1) Symmetric Tree (LC 101)

Pattern: Bottom-up recursion, comparing two subtrees in parallel.

1class Solution:
2    def isSymmetric(self, root):
3        if not root:
4            return True
5        return self.mirror(root.left, root.right)
6
7    def mirror(self, left, right):
8        if not left or not right:
9            return left == right
10        if left.val != right.val:
11            return False
12        return self.mirror(left.left, right.right) and self.mirror(left.right, right.left)

4-2) One Edit Distance (LC 161)

Pattern: Pruning branches early (abs difference > 1), then checking each position.

1class Solution:
2    def isOneEditDistance(self, s, t):
3        m, n = len(s), len(t)
4        if abs(m - n) > 1:
5            return False
6        if m > n:
7            return self.isOneEditDistance(t, s)
8        for i in range(m):
9            if s[i] != t[i]:
10                if m == n:
11                    return s[i + 1:] == t[i + 1:]
12                return s[i:] == t[i + 1:]
13        return m != n

4-3) Merge Two Sorted Lists (LC 21)

Pattern: Simple recursion with local state update.

1class Solution:
2    def mergeTwoLists(self, l1, l2):
3        if not l1 or not l2:
4            return l1 or l2
5        if l1.val < l2.val:
6            l1.next = self.mergeTwoLists(l1.next, l2)
7            return l1
8        else:
9            l2.next = self.mergeTwoLists(l1, l2.next)
10            return l2

4-4) Subtree of Another Tree (LC 572)

Pattern: Any-true status with recursive helper.

1class Solution:
2    def isSubtree(self, root, subRoot):
3        def isSameTree(p, q):
4            if not p and not q:
5                return True
6            if not p or not q:
7                return False
8            return (p.val == q.val and 
9                    isSameTree(p.left, q.left) and 
10                    isSameTree(p.right, q.right))
11        
12        if not root and not subRoot:
13            return True
14        if not root or not subRoot:
15            return False
16        # Use OR: if any recursive call returns True, stop early
17        return (isSameTree(root, subRoot) or 
18                self.isSubtree(root.left, subRoot) or 
19                self.isSubtree(root.right, subRoot))

Java Version:

1public boolean isSubtree(TreeNode root, TreeNode subRoot) {
2    if (root == null) {
3        return false;
4    }
5    if (isIdentical(root, subRoot)) {
6        return true;
7    }
8    return isSubtree(root.left, subRoot) || isSubtree(root.right, subRoot);
9}
10
11private boolean isIdentical(TreeNode node1, TreeNode node2) {
12    if (node1 == null || node2 == null) {
13        return node1 == null && node2 == null;
14    }
15    return node1.val == node2.val && 
16           isIdentical(node1.left, node2.left) && 
17           isIdentical(node1.right, node2.right);
18}
